title: Understanding Docker Basics
context: You are new to Docker and want to understand its foundational concepts.
task_steps: Install Docker on your system.
Explore the foundational concepts and workflows of Docker.
validation_target: Demonstrate knowledge of Docker basics.

title: Configuring Environment Variables in Dockerfiles
context: You need to configure environment variables in a Dockerfile for a project.
task_steps: Identify the correct syntax for setting environment variables in a Dockerfile.
Understand how to use bash modifiers within environment variables.
Apply variable replacements in a Dockerfile using various patterns.
validation_target: Correctly configure environment variables and apply bash modifiers in a Dockerfile.

title: Changing the Default Shell in Docker
context: You need to change the default shell used in a Docker container.
task_steps: Identify the command to change the default shell in a Dockerfile.
Explain the purpose of changing the default shell.
validation_target: Change the default shell in a Dockerfile.

title: Handling Cache Invalidation in Docker Builds
context: You are working on a Docker build process and need to manage cache invalidation.
task_steps: Explain why the cache for RUN instructions is not invalidated by default.
Describe how to invalidate the cache for RUN instructions.
Discuss the impact of using ADD and COPY instructions on cache invalidation.
validation_target: Manage cache invalidation effectively in Docker builds.

title: Using Bind Mounts in Docker
context: You need to bind files or directories to a Docker build container for a project.
task_steps: Identify the command to use a bind mount in a Dockerfile.
Explain the behavior of bind mounts in terms of read-only access.
Discuss the implications of using bind mounts for project development.
validation_target: Configure and understand the use of bind mounts in Docker.

title: Using Cache Mounts in Docker
context: You are optimizing a Docker build process and need to use cache mounts for performance improvements.
task_steps: Identify the command to use a cache mount in a Dockerfile.
Explain the persistence of cache directories between builder invocations.
Discuss the options for sharing cache directories among parallel builds.
Describe the potential risks and benefits of using cache mounts.
validation_target: Configure and understand the use of cache mounts in Docker for optimized builds.

title: Understanding ENTRYPOINT and CMD Interaction
context: Given the interaction between ENTRYPOINT and CMD in Dockerfiles, understand how they work together to define the command executed when running a container.
task_steps: Explain the purpose of ENTRYPOINT and CMD in Dockerfiles.
Describe the rules governing the interaction between ENTRYPOINT and CMD.
Provide an example of a Dockerfile with both ENTRYPOINT and CMD, and explain the resulting command executed when running the container.
validation_target: Correctly describe the interaction between ENTRYPOINT and CMD and provide a valid example.

title: Volume Specification in Dockerfile
context: Learn about the correct way to specify volumes in a Dockerfile, especially on Windows-based containers.
task_steps: Identify the correct format for specifying volumes in a Dockerfile.
Explain why the destination of a volume inside the container must be a non-existing or empty directory or a drive other than C: on Windows-based containers.
Discuss the implications of changing volumes from within the Dockerfile on Windows-based containers.
validation_target: Correctly identify the correct format and implications of volume specification in a Dockerfile.

title: BuildKit and Platform ARGs
context: Explore the use of BuildKit and its built-in platform ARGs in Docker builds.
task_steps: Describe the automatic setting of platform-related ARG variables in BuildKit.
Explain the difference between global and local scope for these ARG variables in the build process.
Provide an example of how to redefine a global ARG variable in a build stage.
Discuss the impact of these ARG variables on build caching.
validation_target: Correctly understand and apply the use of BuildKit and platform ARGs in Docker builds.

title: Scenario: Copy Files Across Build Stages
context: You are working on a multi-stage Docker image where you need to copy specific files from one stage to another.
task_steps: Identify the source and target stages in the Dockerfile.
Write the appropriate ONBUILD instruction to copy files from the source stage to the target stage.
Ensure the source stage is defined in the Dockerfile where the ONBUILD gets triggered.
Test the Docker image to verify the files have been copied correctly.
validation_target: Correctly configured ONBUILD instruction for file copying between stages

title: Scenario: Expose External IP for Stateless Application
context: You need to expose an external IP address to access a stateless application running in a Kubernetes cluster.
task_steps: Identify the service type needed for external access.
Create a Kubernetes service with the appropriate type (e.g., LoadBalancer).
Deploy the application and ensure it is accessible via the external IP.
validation_target: External IP exposed for the application

title: Scenario: Deploy Stateful Application Using StatefulSets
context: You need to deploy a stateful application like WordPress with MySQL using Kubernetes StatefulSets.
task_steps: Define the StatefulSet manifest for the WordPress application.
Configure persistent volumes for the MySQL database.
Deploy the StatefulSet and ensure the application and database are accessible.
Verify the application statefulness by checking the pod order and stable network identifiers.
validation_target: WordPress and MySQL deployed with StatefulSets and persistent volumes

title: Scenario: Manage Infrastructure with Terraform
context: You are tasked with managing infrastructure using Terraform.
task_steps: Describe the infrastructure using Terraform's configuration language.
Initialize the Terraform workspace and apply the configuration.
Verify the infrastructure matches the described configuration.
Update the configuration and reapply to see changes reflected in the infrastructure.
validation_target: Infrastructure matches the described configuration in Terraform

title: Scenario: Create Inventories in INI or YAML Format
context: You need to create an inventory for Ansible to manage nodes.
task_steps: Choose between INI or YAML format based on the number of managed nodes.
Create an inventory file with meaningful and unique group names.
Ensure group names are case-sensitive and avoid spaces, hyphens, and leading numbers.
Group hosts logically by their purpose, location, and stage.
validation_target: Correctly formatted inventory file for Ansible

title: Identify Cloud Technologies
context: You are a DevOps engineer looking to enhance your knowledge of cloud technologies.
task_steps: Read the provided information about cloud technologies.
Identify the cloud technologies mentioned.
validation_target: List of cloud technologies

title: Explore Training Options
context: You are interested in learning more about training and certifications offered by Red Hat.
task_steps: Read the provided information about training and certification options.
List the available training and certification options.
validation_target: List of training and certification options

title: Understand Desired State and Idempotency
context: You need to understand the concept of desired state and idempotency in Ansible.
task_steps: Read the provided information about desired state and idempotency.
Explain what idempotency means in the context of Ansible playbooks.
Provide an example of a non-idempotent task in Ansible.
validation_target: Explanation of idempotency and example of a non-idempotent task

title: Run Playbooks in Check Mode
context: You want to understand how to run playbooks in check mode to test them safely.
task_steps: Read the provided information about running playbooks in check mode.
Describe the purpose of check mode.
List the steps to run a playbook in check mode using the ansible-playbook command.
validation_target: Description of check mode and steps to run a playbook in check mode

title: Verify Playbooks
context: You need to ensure your playbooks are free from errors before running them in production.
task_steps: Read the provided information about verifying playbooks.
List the different options available for verifying playbooks using the ansible-playbook command.
Explain the purpose of each verification option listed.
validation_target: List of verification options and their purposes

title: Handling Undefined Variables
context: You are working on an Ansible playbook where some variables might be undefined.
task_steps: Identify a variable that might be undefined in your playbook.
Decide whether to provide a default value or make the variable optional.
Implement the chosen solution in your playbook.
validation_target: Ensure the playbook runs without errors related to undefined variables.

title: Providing Default Values
context: You are creating a Jinja2 template for an Ansible playbook.
task_steps: Identify a variable that might not be defined in the template.
Use the Jinja2 'default' filter to provide a default value for the variable.
Test the template to ensure it works correctly with and without the variable being defined.
validation_target: The template should render correctly with both defined and undefined variables.

title: Making Variables Optional
context: You are configuring an Ansible role and want to make certain variables optional.
task_steps: Identify the variables that should be optional in your role.
Set the default value of these variables to the special variable 'omit'.
Test the role to ensure it works correctly with and without these variables being defined.
validation_target: The role should function correctly even if some optional variables are undefined.

title: Defining Mandatory Values
context: You are configuring an Ansible playbook to handle undefined variables gracefully.
task_steps: Configure Ansible to ignore undefined variables by setting DEFAULT_UNDEFINED_VAR_BEHAVIOR to false.
Identify variables that must be defined in your playbook.
Use the undef() function to set these variables as mandatory.
Test the playbook to ensure it fails when these variables are undefined.
validation_target: The playbook should fail when mandatory variables are undefined.

title: Managing Data Types
context: You are working with a variable in an Ansible playbook that needs to be converted to a different data type.
task_steps: Identify the variable and the required data type.
Use the appropriate Ansible filters to convert the variable to the desired data type.
Test the playbook to ensure the variable is used correctly in subsequent tasks.
validation_target: The variable should be used correctly in the playbook after conversion.

title: Discovering Data Types
context: You are debugging an Ansible playbook and need to determine the data type of a variable.
task_steps: Identify the variable whose data type you need to check.
Use the ansible.builtin.type_debug filter to inspect the variable's data type.
Analyze the output to understand the underlying Python type of the variable.
Use the data type information to debug or modify the playbook as needed.
validation_target: You should be able to determine the correct data type of the variable.

title: Transforming Strings into Lists
context: Given a string that needs to be split into a list using a specific delimiter.
task_steps: Identify the delimiter used to split the string.
Apply the `ansible.builtin.split` filter to the string.
Verify the resulting list contains the correct elements.
validation_target: The resulting list should contain the correct elements separated by the specified delimiter.

title: Transforming Dictionaries into Lists
context: Given a dictionary that needs to be transformed into a list of key-value pairs.
task_steps: Identify the dictionary to be transformed.
Apply the `ansible.builtin.dict2items` filter to the dictionary.
Verify the resulting list contains the correct key-value pairs.
validation_target: The resulting list should contain the correct key-value pairs.

title: Transforming Lists into Dictionaries
context: Given a list of key-value pairs that need to be transformed into a dictionary.
task_steps: Identify the list to be transformed.
Apply the `ansible.builtin.items2dict` filter to the list.
Verify the resulting dictionary contains the correct key-value pairs.
If necessary, use the `key_name` and `value_name` arguments to customize the keys and values.
validation_target: The resulting dictionary should contain the correct key-value pairs.

title: Forcing Data Type Conversion
context: Given a variable that needs to be converted to a specific data type.
task_steps: Identify the variable to be converted.
Use the appropriate casting method to force the data type conversion.
Verify the variable now holds the correct data type.
validation_target: The variable should hold the correct data type.

title: Formatting Data: YAML and JSON
context: Given a data structure that needs to be formatted as YAML or JSON.
task_steps: Identify the data structure to be formatted.
Apply the `ansible.builtin.to_json` or `ansible.builtin.to_yaml` filter to the data structure.
Verify the formatted output matches the expected YAML or JSON structure.
If desired, apply the `ansible.builtin.to_nice_json` or `ansible.builtin.to_nice_yaml` filter for human-readable output.
validation_target: The formatted output should match the expected YAML or JSON structure.

title: Handling Unicode Support in JSON
context: Given a data structure containing Unicode characters that needs to be converted to JSON.
task_steps: Identify the data structure containing Unicode characters.
Apply the `ansible.builtin.to_json` filter to the data structure.
Pass the `ensure_ascii=False` parameter to the filter to preserve Unicode characters.
Verify the resulting JSON string contains the correct Unicode characters.
If needed, parse multi-document YAML strings using the `ansible.builtin.from_yaml_all` filter.
validation_target: The resulting JSON string should contain the correct Unicode characters.

title: Using Random Filters in Ansible
context: You are working on a project where you need to randomly select items and generate random numbers.
task_steps: Use the `random` filter to select a random item from a list of items.
Generate a random number between 0 (inclusive) and 10 (exclusive).
Generate a random number between 0 and 100 in steps of 10.
Generate a random number between 1 and 100 in steps of 10.
validation_target: Randomly selected item and generated numbers

title: Performing Mathematical Operations in Ansible
context: You need to perform various mathematical operations using Ansible filters.
task_steps: Calculate the natural logarithm of a number.
Calculate the base 10 logarithm of a number.
Raise a number to the power of another number.
Find the square root of a number.
validation_target: Calculated results

title: Parsing Network Device CLI Output
context: You are tasked with converting the output of a network device CLI command into a structured JSON format.
task_steps: Create a YAML spec file to parse the output of the `show vlan` command.
Use the `parse_cli` filter to convert the CLI output into JSON.
Modify the spec file to parse the output into a hash value.
Break the `show vlan` command into blocks for parsing.
validation_target: Parsed JSON output

title: Converting Network Device XML Output to JSON
context: You need to convert the XML output of a network device command into a structured JSON format.
task_steps: Create a YAML spec file to parse the output of the `show vlan | display xml` command.
Use the `parse_xml` filter to convert the XML output into JSON.
Modify the spec file to parse the output into a hash value.
Break the `show vlan | display xml` command into blocks for parsing.
validation_target: Parsed JSON output

title: Sorting VLAN Integers in Ansible
context: You are working on a project that requires sorting VLAN integers according to specific rules.
task_steps: Sort a given unsorted list of VLAN integers.
Apply the sorting rules to generate a VLAN list with three or more consecutive VLANs listed with a dash.
Ensure the first line of the list is first_line_len characters long.
Ensure subsequent list lines are other_line_len characters long.
validation_target: Sorted VLAN list

title: Adding Comments to Files Using Ansible
context: You need to add comments to a file using Ansible.
task_steps: Use the `comment` filter to add a comment line starting with `#`.
Add a blank comment line above and below the comment text.
Define a custom comment character for the `comment` filter.
Fully customize the comment style.
validation_target: File with added comments

title: Using Regular Expressions in Ansible
context: You need to perform various operations on strings using regular expressions in Ansible.
task_steps: Identify the correct filter to search for a pattern in a string.
Determine the appropriate filter to find all occurrences of a pattern in a string.
Choose the right filter to replace text in a string based on a regular expression.
Select the proper filter to escape special characters in a regular expression for Python usage.
Pick the suitable filter to escape special characters in a regular expression for POSIX basic usage.
validation_target: Correctly identify and apply the appropriate Ansible filters for string manipulation using regular expressions.

title: String Manipulation in Ansible
context: You need to manipulate strings in Ansible for various purposes.
task_steps: Identify the filter to add quotes around a string for shell usage.
Determine the filter to concatenate a list into a string.
Choose the appropriate filter to split a string into a list.
Select the filter to encode a string into Base64.
validation_target: Correctly identify and apply the appropriate Ansible filters for string manipulation.

title: Date and Time Handling in Ansible
context: You need to handle dates and times in Ansible.
task_steps: Identify the filter to convert a string to a date object.
Determine the filter to format a date using a string.
Choose the appropriate option to set the time zone for the formatted date.
validation_target: Correctly identify and apply the appropriate Ansible filters for date and time handling.

title: Kubernetes Resource Names in Ansible
context: You need to work with Kubernetes resources in Ansible.
task_steps: Identify the filter to get the name of a Kubernetes ConfigMap or Secret including its hash.
Determine how to use the obtained name in a Pod specification.
validation_target: Correctly identify and apply the appropriate Ansible filter for working with Kubernetes resources.

title: List Containment Testing in Ansible
context: You need to test if a list contains a specific value in Ansible.
task_steps: Identify the correct test to check if a list contains a value.
Determine the appropriate test to check if a value is not in a list.
validation_target: Correctly identify and apply the appropriate Ansible tests for list containment.

title: Size Format Testing in Ansible
context: You need to ensure the correct size format is used in Ansible.
task_steps: Identify the function to test human-readable size formats.
Determine the function to test byte sizes.
validation_target: Correctly identify and apply the appropriate Ansible functions for testing size formats.

title: Understanding Task Testing
context: You are working on a project that involves running various tasks in an Ansible playbook.
task_steps: Identify the purpose of the testing tasks mentioned in the dataset.
Describe how these tasks help in checking the status of tasks.
validation_target: Correctly identify the purpose and benefits of testing tasks in Ansible playbooks.

title: Using Lookup Function
context: You are configuring an Ansible playbook to dynamically populate variables using lookup functions.
task_steps: Explain the usage of the lookup function in Ansible.
Discuss the importance of specifying the lookup plugin name and the wantlist keyword.
Provide an example of how to use the lookup function with a specific plugin.
validation_target: Properly understand and apply the lookup function in Ansible playbooks.

title: Shorthand Query Function
context: You are familiarizing yourself with Ansible's shorthand functions.
task_steps: Compare and contrast the use of the query function and the q function.
Explain when and why one might prefer to use the q function over the query function.
validation_target: Recognize the equivalence and use cases of the query and q functions in Ansible.

title: Iterating Over Simple Lists
context: You are setting up a playbook to perform repeated tasks on a list of hosts.
task_steps: Describe how to write repeated tasks using a simple list of strings in Ansible.
Explain the difference between defining the list directly in the task and referencing a variables file.
validation_target: Configure repeated tasks using a simple list in Ansible playbooks.

title: Task Retrying Mechanism
context: You are troubleshooting a task that fails intermittently in your Ansible playbook.
task_steps: Explain the purpose and usage of the until keyword in Ansible.
Describe the conditions under which a task would succeed using the until keyword.
Provide an example of combining the until keyword with a loop to retry tasks.
validation_target: Implement and debug retry mechanisms in Ansible playbooks.

title: Inventory Looping
context: You are designing an Ansible playbook to target specific hosts within your inventory.
task_steps: Explain how to loop over inventory in an Ansible playbook.
Discuss the use of ansible_play_batch and groups variables for looping.
Provide an example of using the inventory_hostnamest lookup plugin.
validation_target: Configure inventory looping in Ansible playbooks to target specific hosts.

title: Understanding Loop Input Handling in Ansible
context: In Ansible, the loop keyword often requires a list as input, but sometimes the lookup plugin returns a string of comma-separated values.
task_steps: Explain why the loop keyword requires a list as input.
Describe how the lookup plugin returns a string of comma-separated values by default.
Discuss the introduction of the query function in Ansible 2.5 and its benefits.
Provide an example of how to force the lookup plugin to return a list.
validation_target: Correctly identify the differences between loop and lookup and the benefits of using query.

title: Limiting Output in Loops
context: When working with complex data structures in Ansible, the output of loops can be extensive.
task_steps: Explain the purpose of the label directive in loop_control.
Describe how to limit the output to specific fields in a loop.
Provide an example of using label to limit the output in a loop.
validation_target: Properly use the label directive to control loop output.

title: Controlling Loop Execution Time
context: Sometimes it is necessary to control the time between the execution of each item in a loop.
task_steps: Explain what the pause directive does in Ansible.
Describe how to use the pause directive with loop_control.
Provide an example of using the pause directive in a loop.
validation_target: Understand and apply the pause directive to control loop execution time.

title: Using Extended Loop Variables in Ansible
context: Starting from Ansible 2.8, extended loop information can be accessed using the extended option.
task_steps: Explain what the extended option does in loop control.
Describe the information exposed by the extended option.
Discuss the memory considerations when using the extended option.
Provide an example of disabling the ansible_loop.allitems item using the extended option.
validation_target: Correctly use the extended option to access additional loop information.

title: Accessing Loop Variable Names in Ansible
context: Starting from Ansible 2.8, the name of the loop variable can be accessed using the ansible_loop_var variable.
task_steps: Explain how to access the name of the loop variable using ansible_loop_var.
Discuss the implications for role authors when writing roles that allow loops.
Provide an example of gathering the loop variable value through ansible_loop_var.
validation_target: Properly use ansible_loop_var to access loop variable names.

title: Nesting Loops Using include_tasks in Ansible
context: Nested loops can be achieved in Ansible using include_tasks, but there are potential issues with variable overwriting.
task_steps: Explain how to nest two looping tasks using include_tasks.
Discuss the default behavior of loop variables in nested loops.
Identify the issue with the default behavior of loop variables in nested loops.
Describe how to specify different loop variables for each loop using loop_var with loop_control.
Provide an example of correctly nesting loops using include_tasks and loop_control.
validation_target: Successfully implement nested loops without variable overwriting issues.

title: Identifying Non-delegatable Tasks
context: Understanding which tasks cannot be delegated in Ansible.
task_steps: Identify which tasks cannot be delegated.
Explain why these tasks cannot be delegated.
validation_target: Correct identification of non-delegatable tasks and understanding of the reason.

title: Explaining Templating in Delegation Context
context: Understanding how templating works in the context of task delegation in Ansible.
task_steps: Describe how templating works during task delegation.
Identify which variables are consumed from the delegated host.
Explain the usage of hostvars[inventory_hostname]['varname'] in the context of templating.
validation_target: Correct explanation of templating behavior during task delegation.

title: Handling Concurrency Issues in Delegation
context: Understanding and managing concurrency issues in task delegation within Ansible.
task_steps: Explain the default behavior of task execution in Ansible.
Identify the potential issue with concurrent execution in delegation.
Propose at least two methods to handle concurrency issues in task delegation.
validation_target: Correct identification of concurrency issues and proposed solutions.

title: Implementing Basic Conditionals with when
context: Creating basic conditionals in Ansible playbooks.
task_steps: Create a task with a when clause.
Ensure the when clause is a raw Jinja2 expression.
Run the task and observe the outcome based on the when clause.
validation_target: Correct implementation of basic conditionals.

title: Using Conditional Statements Based on ansible_facts
context: Applying conditionals based on host facts in Ansible.
task_steps: List common facts used in conditional statements.
Create a conditional statement using a fact.
Run the playbook and verify the conditional logic based on the fact.
validation_target: Correct application of conditionals based on host facts.

title: Conditional Execution Based on Registered Variables
context: Executing tasks based on the outcome of previous tasks in Ansible.
task_steps: Register a variable in a task.
Use the registered variable in a conditional statement.
Run the playbook and observe the outcome based on the registered variable.
Handle scenarios where the registered variable is empty or skipped.
validation_target: Correct handling of registered variables in conditional statements.

title: Conditional Logic Based on Variables
context: You are working on a playbook where you need to conditionally execute tasks based on the value of a variable.
task_steps: Define a variable in the playbook or inventory that can take different values (e.g., 'yes', 'no', '1', '0').
Use the bool filter to ensure the variable is treated as a boolean.
Create a conditional block that runs a specific task only if the variable evaluates to true.
Test the conditional logic by setting the variable to different values and observing the outcome.
validation_target: The conditional block should correctly execute the task only when the variable evaluates to true.

title: Conditional Execution in Loops
context: You have a list of servers that need to be configured, but some of them might not meet certain criteria.
task_steps: Create a loop that iterates over a list of servers.
Inside the loop, use an awhen statement to conditionally execute a task based on a fact about each server.
Use the default filter to handle cases where the loop variable is undefined.
Test the loop and conditional logic to ensure tasks are executed only on servers that meet the criteria.
validation_target: Tasks should be executed only on servers that meet the specified criteria, and the loop should gracefully handle undefined variables.

title: Loading Custom Facts
context: You need to gather custom facts about hosts before running any tasks.
task_steps: Develop a custom fact-gathering module.
Call the custom fact-gathering module at the beginning of your playbook.
Use the gathered facts in subsequent tasks to conditionally execute actions.
Verify that the custom facts are available and used correctly in the playbook.
validation_target: Custom facts should be successfully gathered and used in subsequent tasks.

title: Conditional Reuse in Playbooks
context: You are reusing tasks, playbooks, or roles in your Ansible workflow and need to apply conditional logic.
task_steps: Identify the reusable artifact (playbook, role, or task file) that needs conditional execution.
Apply conditional logic to the reusable artifact using includes or imports.
Ensure the conditional logic works correctly for both dynamic and static reuse scenarios.
Test the conditional logic across different use cases to confirm its correctness.
validation_target: Conditional logic should work correctly for both dynamic and static reuse scenarios.

title: Selecting Variables, Files, or Templates Based on Facts
context: You need to configure hosts differently based on their operating system or other facts.
task_steps: Gather facts about the hosts using a custom fact-gathering module.
Use the gathered facts to conditionally select variables, files, or templates for each host.
Ensure the selected variables, files, or templates are correctly applied to the hosts.
Test the configuration across different hosts to verify the correct selection.
validation_target: Variables, files, or templates should be correctly selected and applied based on the host facts.

title: Debugging Conditional Statements
context: You suspect a conditional statement in your playbook is not working as expected.
task_steps: Identify the conditional statement that is not behaving as intended.
Add a debug statement to the playbook to inspect the condition and its evaluation.
Run the playbook and observe the debug output to understand the condition's evaluation.
Adjust the conditional statement based on the debug output and re-run the playbook to verify the fix.
validation_target: The conditional statement should now behave as intended, and the debug output should reflect the correct evaluation.

title: Understanding Commonly Used Ansible Facts
context: You are working on a project that requires setting up a new environment using Ansible.
task_steps: Identify the purpose of the `ansible_facts['distribution_major_version']` fact.
Explain how this fact can be used in conditional statements.
validation_target: Correctly identify the purpose and usage of the `ansible_facts['distribution_major_version']` fact.

title: Grouping Tasks with Blocks
context: You are tasked with writing an Ansible playbook to manage a server's configuration.
task_steps: Describe the purpose of using blocks in Ansible playbooks.
Explain how the `when` statement works within a block.
Discuss the significance of `ignore_errors` in the context of blocks.
validation_target: Properly understand and articulate the benefits and usage of blocks in Ansible playbooks.

title: Error Handling with Blocks
context: You are developing a complex Ansible playbook that needs robust error handling.
task_steps: Describe the purpose and functionality of the `rescue` and `always` sections within a block.
Explain how the `rescue` section affects the overall success status of a task in the block.
Discuss the implications of using `rescue` and `always` sections on the `max_fail_percentage` and `any_errors_fatal` configurations.
validation_target: Demonstrate a deep understanding of error handling mechanisms in Ansible playbooks.

title: Notifying Handlers with Loops
context: You are configuring a service that requires restarting components based on file changes.
task_steps: Explain how tasks can use loops to notify handlers in Ansible.
Discuss the conditions under which handlers are triggered in the presence of loops.
Provide an example of how multiple handlers can be triggered by a single task change.
validation_target: Understand the interaction between loops and handlers in Ansible playbooks.

title: Order of Handler Insertion in Playbooks
context: You are managing a large-scale infrastructure with multiple roles and dependencies.
task_steps: Describe the global scope for handlers in Ansible playbooks.
List the order in which handlers are inserted into the play and explain the implications of handler naming conflicts.
Provide an example of how the insertion order affects the execution of handlers in a complex playbook.
validation_target: Comprehend the intricacies of handler management in Ansible playbooks, especially in complex scenarios.

title: Controlling When Handlers Run
context: Understanding the default behavior of handlers and how to control their execution.
task_steps: Explain the default behavior of handlers in Ansible.
Describe the order in which notified handlers are executed.
Discuss the purpose of the `meta:flush_handlers` task and when it should be used.
validation_target: Correctly describe the default behavior and control mechanisms for handlers.

title: Defining When Tasks Change
context: Understanding the `changed_when` keyword and its usage.
task_steps: Explain the purpose of the `changed_when` keyword.
Provide an example of how to use `changed_when` in a task to notify a handler.
validation_target: Correctly identify and explain the use of `changed_when`.

title: Using Variables with Handlers
context: Understanding how to properly use variables in handlers without causing playbook failures.
task_steps: Explain why placing variables directly in handler names can cause issues.
Describe the correct way to use variables in handlers.
Discuss the limitations of using templates in handler names.
validation_target: Correctly identify best practices for using variables in handlers.

title: Includes and Imports in Handlers
context: Understanding the differences between dynamic and static includes in handlers.
task_steps: Explain the difference between `include_task` and `import_task` in the context of handlers.
Discuss the implications of notifying a dynamic include as a handler.
Describe what happens when a static include is notified as a handler.

title: Identify Common Shell Scripting Errors

title: Detect Misused Commands

validation_target: Correctly identify and explain the misuse of commands in the provided code snippets.

title: Spot Common Beginner's Syntax Errors

validation_target: Correctly identify and explain the syntax errors in the provided code snippets.

title: Recognize Data and Typing Errors
context: You are examining a shell script for data and typing errors.
task_steps: Identify and explain the error in the assignment `args="$@"`.
Explain why the following assignment is incorrect: `files=(foo bar); echo "$files"`.
Describe the issue with the associative array declaration `declare -A arr=(foo bar)`.
validation_target: Correctly identify and explain the data and typing errors in the provided code snippets.

title: Understand Network Policies
context: Learn about network policies in Kubernetes and how they control traffic between pods.
task_steps: Explain the two types of Pod isolation: egress and ingress.
Describe the three different identifiers used in NetworkPolicies: podSelector, namespaceSelector, and ipBlock.
Identify the conditions under which a NetworkPolicy has no effect.
validation_target: Correctly identify the types of Pod isolation and the conditions under which a NetworkPolicy does not take effect.

title: Start Minikube with Calico Plugin
context: Set up a Kubernetes cluster with the Calico network plugin using minikube.
task_steps: Stop and delete the existing minikube cluster.
Start a new minikube cluster with the Calico plugin and version 1.23.9.
Verify that the Calico plugin is running.
validation_target: A running minikube cluster with the Calico network plugin.

title: View Details of a Network Policy
context: Inspect a specific NetworkPolicy in a Kubernetes cluster.
task_steps: Use `kubectl describe` to view the details of the network policy named `mynetpol`.
validation_target: Correctly display the YAML configuration of the network policy.

title: Implement Default Deny All Traffic
context: Configure a NetworkPolicy to deny all ingress and egress traffic by default.
task_steps: Create a NetworkPolicy with a default deny rule for both ingress and egress traffic.
Apply the NetworkPolicy to the desired namespace.
Verify that the NetworkPolicy is effective by attempting to access a service from a pod without the required labels.
Remove the NetworkPolicy to revert to the default behavior.
validation_target: A NetworkPolicy that effectively denies all traffic by default.

title: Declare a Network Policy
context: Set up a NetworkPolicy to control traffic to a specific service.
task_steps: Create a Kubernetes cluster in minikube with Calico enabled.
Confirm that Calico is up and running.
Deploy a `webapp` using the `httpd` image.
Expose the `webapp` deployment on port 80.
Create a `busybox` Pod and connect an interactive shell.
Run a command to check connectivity to the `webapp` service.
Limit access to the `webapp` service so that only pods with the label `tier=frontend` can access it.
Verify the effectiveness of the NetworkPolicy by attempting to access the `webapp` service from a `busybox` pod without the `tier=frontend` label.
Create another `busybox` Pod with the `tier=frontend` label and verify its ability to access the `webapp` service.
Delete the created resources and revert to a cluster without Calico.
validation_target: A NetworkPolicy that restricts access to the `webapp` service based on pod labels.

title: Debug Ingress Setup
context: Given a Kubernetes environment setup with an Ingress resource, you need to identify and fix issues without creating new resources.
task_steps: Identify the current state of the environment.
Debug the Ingress configuration to ensure the application is accessible at `ckad-bootcamp.local`.
Resolve any issues found without creating new resources.
validation_target: Ensure the application is accessible at `ckad-bootcamp.local`.

title: Configure Network Policies
context: Create network policies to control communication between Pods across different Namespaces.
task_steps: Define network policies for communication within the same Namespace.
Allow `webapp` Pod in `pup` Namespace to communicate with `microservice` Pod in `cat` Namespace.
Enable DNS resolution on UDP/TCP port 53 for all Pods in all Namespaces.
validation_target: Verify that the network policies are correctly applied and enforced.

title: Deployment Variables in Kubernetes
context: You are working on a Kubernetes cluster and need to manage environment variables for your deployments.
task_steps: Create a `db` Deployment using the `mysql` image.
Troubleshoot and fix any deployment issues to ensure the `STATUS` is running.
View more details of the Deployment and note where the environment variable (env-var) is specified.
Review the Deployment in YAML form and note how the env-var is specified.
Create a `db` Pod with an appropriate environment variable specified.
Confirm the Pod is running as expected.
View more details of the Pod and note where the env-var is specified.
Review the Pod in YAML form and note how the env-var is specified.
Delete the created resources.
validation_target: Ensure the Deployment and Pod are running with the correct environment variables.

title: Understanding ConfigMaps in Kubernetes
context: You are learning about ConfigMaps in Kubernetes and their usage.
task_steps: Understand what ConfigMaps are and their purpose in Kubernetes.
Learn how to create a ConfigMap from a directory.
Learn how to create a ConfigMap from literal values.
validation_target: Correctly understand and apply the creation of ConfigMaps.

title: Creating ConfigMap from File or Directory
context: You need to create a ConfigMap from a file or directory.
task_steps: Use the `kubectl create configmap` command to create a ConfigMap named `mycm` from a specified file or directory.
validation_target: Successfully create a ConfigMap from a file or directory.

title: Creating ConfigMap from Literal Values
context: You need to create a ConfigMap from literal values.
task_steps: Use the `kubectl create configmap` command to create a ConfigMap named `mycm` from literal values.
validation_target: Successfully create a ConfigMap from literal values.

title: Displaying Details of ConfigMap
context: You need to inspect a ConfigMap in Kubernetes.
task_steps: Use the `kubectl describe cm` command to display details of the ConfigMap `mycm`.
Use the `kubectl get cm` command to display the ConfigMap `mycm` in YAML format.
validation_target: Successfully display the details of the ConfigMap.

title: Using Specific Keys from ConfigMap
context: You need to use specific keys from a ConfigMap in a Kubernetes deployment.
task_steps: Use the `kubectl set env` command to set environment variables in a deployment using keys from the ConfigMap `mycm`.
validation_target: Successfully use specific keys from a ConfigMap in a Kubernetes deployment.

title: Remove Environment Variable from Deployment
context: Given a deployment named 'web' with an environment variable 'KEY1', remove it.
task_steps: Use kubectl to remove the environment variable 'KEY1' from the deployment 'web'.
validation_target: Ensure that the environment variable 'KEY1' is removed from the deployment 'web'.

title: ConfigMaps as Environment Variables
context: Create and use ConfigMaps to manage environment variables for a MySQL deployment.
task_steps: Create a `file.env` file with environment variables for MySQL.
Create a ConfigMap `mycm-file` from the `file.env` file using `--from-file`.
Create another ConfigMap `mycm-env` from the `file.env` file using `--from-env-file`.
Compare the details of both ConfigMaps and their YAML forms.
Create two deployments, one using `mycm-file` and the other using `mycm-env` as environment variables.
Review the manifest files to ensure the environment variables are correctly configured.
Identify which ConfigMap was used for the working deployment and why.
Create a new deployment with two environment variables from the working ConfigMap.
Connect to a pod from the new deployment and confirm the environment variables using `printenv`.
Create a pod with environment variables from the working ConfigMap and confirm them using `printenv`.
Delete all created resources.
validation_target: Ensure that the environment variables are correctly set in the deployments and pods, and identify the correct ConfigMap usage.

title: Copy Working Environment Variables to Pod Specification
context: Copy the working environment variables from a deployment to a pod specification to avoid adding unnecessary fields.
task_steps: Apply the working deployment configuration.
Get the pod name from the deployment.
Execute a shell in the pod and confirm the environment variables using `printenv`.
Create a pod specification using `kubectl run` with the environment variables copied from the deployment.
Apply the pod specification and confirm the environment variables are set.
Delete all created resources.
validation_target: Ensure that the environment variables are correctly set in the pod specification and the pod.

title: Mounting ConfigMaps to Pods
context: Use ConfigMaps to manage environment variables for a MySQL deployment by mounting them as volumes.
task_steps: Create a `file.env` file with an environment variable for MySQL.
Create a ConfigMap `mycm` from the `file.env` file.
Create a pod specification with the MySQL image and mount the ConfigMap as a volume.
Create the pod and confirm the environment variable is set in the container.
Create a file and a ConfigMap from it.
Create a `webserver` deployment with the appropriate image and mount the file to the DocumentRoot via ConfigMap.
Connect to the container and confirm the file is being served.
Delete all created resources.
validation_target: Ensure that the environment variables are correctly set in the container via the mounted ConfigMap and the file is being served.

title: Create a ConfigMap from a local HTML file and deploy a simple web server using Kubernetes
context: Given a local HTML file, you need to create a ConfigMap and deploy a simple web server using Kubernetes.
task_steps: Create a directory named 'html'
Create an HTML file with a welcome message
Create a ConfigMap from the HTML file
Create a deployment YAML file for a web server
Fix the indentation of the YAML file
Deploy the web server using the corrected YAML file
Verify the deployment and pod status
Check the contents of the HTML file in the pod
Forward the port of the pod to access the web server
Access the web server through the forwarded port
Clean up the resources
validation_target: The HTML file should be accessible via the web server and the deployment should be successful

title: Create a TLS secret from certificate and key files
context: Given paths to a certificate and key file, create a TLS secret using kubectl.
task_steps: Use kubectl to create a TLS secret from the provided certificate and key files.
validation_target: The TLS secret should be successfully created

title: Create a secret from a file for SSH private key
context: Given a path to an SSH private key file, create a secret using kubectl.
task_steps: Use kubectl to create a secret from the provided SSH private key file.
validation_target: The secret should be successfully created

title: Create a secret from environment variables for passwords
context: Given a password, create a secret using kubectl that sets it as an environment variable.
task_steps: Use kubectl to create a secret from the provided password and set it as an environment variable.
validation_target: The secret should be successfully created

title: Create a secret for Docker registry credentials
context: Given a Docker registry username, password, email, and server address, create a secret using kubectl for Docker registry credentials.
task_steps: Use kubectl to create a secret for Docker registry credentials with the provided information.
validation_target: The secret should be successfully created

title: View details of a secret
context: Given a secret name, use kubectl to view its details.
task_steps: Use kubectl to describe the secret and retrieve its details
Use kubectl to get the secret in YAML format
validation_target: The secret details should be displayed correctly

title: Create a ConfigMap and a Secret for a Deployment
context: The latest Bootcamp cohort have requested a new database in the `rig` Namespace.
task_steps: Create a ConfigMap named `db-config` to load the `.env` file.
Create a Secret named `db-secret` with variables `MYSQL_DATABASE=bootcamp` and `MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD="shhhh!"`.
Use the ConfigMap and Secret in a Deployment named `db` running the `mysql:8.0.22` image with container named `mysql`.
Set resource limits for the container as `MY_CPU_LIMIT` and `MY_MEM_LIMIT` environment variables.
Ensure the Pod IP address is available as `MY_POD_IP` environment variable.
validation_target: Verify the ConfigMap and Secret are correctly applied to the Deployment.

title: Configure Liveness and Readiness Probes
context: Configure liveness and readiness probes for a busybox container.
task_steps: Create a Deployment named `myapp` using the busybox image.
Define a liveness probe that checks for the presence of `/tmp/healthy` directory.
Define a readiness probe that checks for the presence of `/tmp/healthy` directory.
Set the initial delay for both probes to 10 seconds.
Set the period for both probes to 10 seconds.
Apply the manifest file to create the Deployment.
Monitor the Pod events for 3-5 minutes.
Delete the created resources.
validation_target: Verify the probes are working as expected and the Pod status changes accordingly.

title: Retrieve Events for a Specific Resource
context: Retrieve events for a specific resource, pod, deployment, etc.
task_steps: Use the `kubectl get events` command with the appropriate field selector to retrieve events for a specific resource.
validation_target: Ensure the correct events are retrieved for the specified resource.

title: Check Deployment Creation Permissions in a Namespace
context: Understanding Kubernetes authorization commands
task_steps: Run the command to check if deployments can be created in the `dev` namespace
validation_target: kubectl auth can-i create deployments --namespace dev

title: Check User Permissions to List Secrets in a Namespace
context: Understanding Kubernetes authorization commands
task_steps: Run the command to check if a specific user can list secrets in the `dev` namespace
validation_target: kubectl auth can-i list secrets --namespace dev --as dave

title: Understanding Service Accounts in Kubernetes
context: Exploring the role and functionality of Service Accounts in Kubernetes
task_steps: Identify the purpose of Service Accounts in Kubernetes
List the conditions under which a Pod is assigned a default Service Account
Describe the contents of the automounted credentials for a ServiceAccount in a Pod
Explain the effect of setting `automountServiceAccountToken: false` on a ServiceAccount
validation_target: Service Accounts in Kubernetes

title: Accessing the Kubernetes API Without kubectl Proxy
context: Understanding how to access the Kubernetes API outside of a Pod
task_steps: Request the ServiceAccount token using YAML
Wait for the token controller to populate the Secret with a token
Use `curl` to access the API with the generated token as credentials
validation_target: Accessing the Kubernetes API without kubectl proxy

title: Accessing the Kubernetes API from Inside a Pod
context: Understanding how to access the Kubernetes API from within a Pod
task_steps: Connect an interactive shell to a container in a running Pod
Use `curl` to access the API at `kubernetes.default.svc/api` with the automounted ServiceAccount credentials
Verify access to the Pods list at `kubernetes.default.svc/api/v1/namespaces/default/pods`
validation_target: Accessing the Kubernetes API from inside a Pod

title: ServiceAccount Permissions Overview
context: Understanding different approaches to granting permissions to ServiceAccounts in Kubernetes.
task_steps: Explain the difference between granting permissions to the default service account and creating a specific service account with roles.
Identify which approach is considered best practice.
validation_target: Correctly explain the differences and identify the best practice.

title: Assigning Service Account to a Deployment
context: Using kubectl to manage service accounts in deployments.
task_steps: Write the command to assign a service account to a deployment.
Identify the variables needed for the command.
validation_target: Correct command and identification of required variables.

title: Creating a Role for Pod Operations
context: Creating a role with specific verbs for pod operations.
task_steps: Write the command to create a role that allows users to perform get, watch, and list on pods.
Identify the necessary parameters for the command.
validation_target: Correct command and identification of required parameters.

title: Granting Namespace-Level Role Permissions
context: Creating a role binding to grant permissions within a namespace.
task_steps: Write the command to create a role binding that grants a role to a user within a specific namespace.
Identify the necessary parameters for the command.
validation_target: Correct command and identification of required parameters.

title: Granting Cluster-Level Role Permissions
context: Creating a cluster role binding to grant permissions across the entire cluster.
task_steps: Write the command to create a cluster role binding that grants a cluster role to a user across the entire cluster.
Identify the necessary parameters for the command.
validation_target: Correct command and identification of required parameters.

title: Granting Cluster-Level Role to a Service Account
context: Creating a role binding to grant a cluster role to a service account within a namespace.
task_steps: Write the command to create a role binding that grants a cluster role to a service account within a specific namespace.
Identify the necessary parameters for the command.
validation_target: Correct command and identification of required parameters.

title: RBAC Permissions for Pod List Access
context: Given the need to access the PodList API from within a Kubernetes Pod using RBAC, you will need to set up the necessary roles and bindings.
task_steps: Create a ServiceAccount named `test-sa`.
Create a Role named `test-role` with permission to list pods in the `default` namespace.
Create a RoleBinding that associates the `test-role` with the `test-sa` in the `default` namespace.
Deploy a Pod using the `test-sa` and ensure it has the necessary permissions to access the PodList API.
Connect to the Pod's interactive shell and use `curl` to access the PodList API.
validation_target: Verify that the Pod can access the PodList API using the specified Role and RoleBinding.

title: Creating a Role YAML File
context: Learn how to create a YAML file for a Kubernetes Role.
task_steps: Use `kubectl` to create a Role named `test-role` with the ability to list pods in the `default` namespace.
Save the output as a YAML file named `lab13-4.yaml`.
validation_target: The `lab13-4.yaml` file should contain the correct Role definition.

title: Creating a ConfigMap YAML File
context: Learn how to create a YAML file for a Kubernetes ConfigMap.
task_steps: Use `kubectl` to create a ConfigMap named `test-cm` with a service account path.
Save the output as a YAML file named `lab13-4.yaml`.
validation_target: The `lab13-4.yaml` file should contain the correct ConfigMap definition.

title: Creating a Pod YAML File
context: Learn how to create a YAML file for a Kubernetes Pod.
task_steps: Use `kubectl` to create a Pod named `test-pod` using the `nginx` image.
Save the output as a YAML file named `lab13-4.yaml`.
validation_target: The `lab13-4.yaml` file should contain the correct Pod definition.

title: Verifying Created Resources
context: Learn how to verify the creation of various Kubernetes resources.
task_steps: List all ServiceAccounts.
Describe the `test-sa` ServiceAccount.
List all Roles.
Describe the `test-role` Role.
List all RoleBindings.
Describe the `test-rolebinding` RoleBinding.
List all ConfigMaps.
Describe the `test-cm` ConfigMap.
List all Pods.
validation_target: Ensure all resources have been correctly created and configured.

title: Accessing Kubernetes API from Within a Pod
context: Learn how to access the Kubernetes API from within a Pod using RBAC.
task_steps: Execute an interactive shell on the `test-pod`.
Extract the token from the service account.
Set the Authorization header using the extracted token.
Use `curl` to access the PodList API.
Use `curl` to access a specific Pod.
Use `curl` to access deployments.
Exit the shell.
validation_target: Successfully access the Kubernetes API from within the Pod using the specified Role and RoleBinding.

title: Install Helm Client on Linux
context: Given a scenario where you need to set up a new development environment for Kubernetes cluster management using Helm.
task_steps: Download the latest version of Helm client for Linux.
Extract the downloaded file.
Install the Helm client in the system's PATH.
Verify the installation of Helm client.
validation_target: Ensure Helm client is installed correctly and can be used to manage Helm charts.

title: Adding a Helm Repository and Installing a Chart
context: In a complex Kubernetes deployment scenario, you need to add a new Helm repository and install a specific chart from it.
task_steps: Add a new Helm repository to your local Helm client.
Update the local Helm repository cache.
Install a specific Helm chart from the newly added repository.
Verify the installation of the chart.
validation_target: Ensure the Helm chart is installed successfully from the new repository.

title: Configure Helm to Use a Specific Registry
context: You are tasked with configuring Helm to use a specific artifact registry for your organization’s Helm charts.
task_steps: Identify the URL of the artifact registry.
Configure Helm to trust the artifact registry’s certificate.
Set the Helm repository to point to the artifact registry.
Test the configuration by listing available charts from the registry.
validation_target: Ensure Helm can access and list charts from the specified artifact registry.

title: Installing and Exploring Helm Charts
context: You are working on a project that involves setting up a new Kubernetes environment and need to install and explore Helm charts.
task_steps: Install Helm
List installed Helm charts
Find and install a chart from ArtifactHUB
View resources created by the Helm chart
Update Helm repo
List installed Helm charts again
List installed Helm repos
View details of installed chart
View status of installed chart
Search for available charts in added Helm repo
validation_target: Ensure all steps are completed successfully and the Helm chart is installed and functioning as expected.

title: Customizing Helm Templates
context: You have downloaded a Helm chart and want to customize its configuration before installing it.
task_steps: Edit the Helm chart's configuration file
Verify the edited configuration
Install the customized Helm chart
View resources created by the Helm chart
List installed Helm charts
List installed Helm repos
validation_target: Ensure the customization is applied correctly and the Helm chart installs without errors.

title: Using Kustomize to Manage Resources
context: You need to manage multiple Kubernetes resources using Kustomize in a complex deployment scenario.
task_steps: Create a `service.yaml` resource file
Create a `deployment.yaml` resource file
Create a `kustomization.yaml` file with name prefix/suffix and common labels
Apply the Kustomization file to create the resources
Review the resources created to ensure they match the expectations
validation_target: Ensure all resources are created as per the Kustomization file and the prefix/suffix and labels are applied correctly.

title: Understand and Implement the Operator Pattern
context: Learn about the operator pattern and its application in managing Kubernetes resources.
task_steps: Read the provided links about the operator pattern and custom resources.
Identify a suitable application for an operator in your environment.
Choose a pre-existing operator from OperatorHub.io or write a simple operator.
Deploy the chosen operator or your custom operator in the Kubernetes cluster.
Use the operator to manage a specific resource or application in the cluster.
validation_target: The operator should successfully manage the specified resource or application.

title: Environment Setup
context: Setting up the development environment for Kubernetes operations.
task_steps: Setup `kubectl` with necessary aliases and dry-run options.
Configure the text editor with preferred settings.
Switch to the specified Kubernetes cluster for the question.
Set a variable for the namespace to be used in the question.
validation_target: Ensure `kubectl` and text editor configurations are correct.

title: Using Variables and Aliases
context: Creating and using variables and aliases in shell commands.
task_steps: Create a deployment using an image and save the output to a file.
Use the `kubectl` alias and dry-run option correctly.
Store the namespace in a variable for subsequent commands.
validation_target: Verify the command creates the deployment and saves it to the correct file.

title: Text Editor Proficiency
context: Improving efficiency in text editors for DevOps tasks.
task_steps: Perform bulk replacements in the text editor.
Indent multiple lines using visual selection in the text editor.
Undo and redo changes in the text editor.
validation_target: Demonstrate the ability to perform bulk changes, indentation, and undo/redo actions in the text editor.

title: Exam Questions Approach
context: Strategizing the order of answering questions based on their scores.
task_steps: Review all questions before starting the exam.
Create a grid to prioritize questions based on their scores.
Decide the order of answering questions to maximize points.
validation_target: Create a strategy for answering questions based on their scores.

title: Node Failure Impact
context: Understanding the impact of node failures on a Kubernetes cluster.
task_steps: Explain what happens when a master node fails.
Describe the behavior of the cluster when a worker node fails.
Discuss the rescheduling process for pods on failed nodes.
validation_target: Correctly describe the resilience of the Kubernetes cluster to node failures.

title: Understanding DNS in Kubernetes
context: DNS plays a crucial role in Kubernetes for service discovery.
task_steps: Explain how DNS works in a Kubernetes cluster.
Identify the components involved in DNS service discovery.
Describe how to access a service within the same namespace.
validation_target: Correctly describe the DNS mechanism in Kubernetes.

title: Building a High Availability Cluster
context: Ensuring high availability in a Kubernetes cluster involves multiple components.
task_steps: Identify the stateful and stateless parts of a Kubernetes cluster.
Describe the role of the etcd cluster in an HA setup.
Outline the steps to create an HA cluster.
validation_target: Properly outline the components and steps for creating an HA Kubernetes cluster.

title: Network Isolation in Namespaces
context: Network policies can be used to control traffic between namespaces.
task_steps: Explain the purpose of network policies in Kubernetes.
Identify the tools needed for network isolation.
Describe how to apply network policies to isolate namespaces.
validation_target: Correctly explain the use of network policies for isolating namespaces.

title: Choosing Between Replication Controllers and Deployments
context: Replication controllers and deployments serve similar purposes but differ in features.
task_steps: Compare the features of replication controllers and deployments.
Identify the recommended practice for managing application instances in Kubernetes.
Explain why using deployments over replication controllers is preferred.
validation_target: Correctly identify the preferred object for managing application instances in Kubernetes.

title: Checking the Status of a Deployment
context: Monitoring the status of a deployment is essential for maintaining application health.
task_steps: List the fields that indicate a successful deployment completion.
Describe how to check the status of a deployment using kubectl.
Identify the conditions under which a deployment is considered complete.
validation_target: Correctly identify the status indicators for a completed deployment.

title: Updating Pods Without Downtime
context: Efficiently updating pods while minimizing downtime is critical for maintaining application availability.
task_steps: Describe the process of updating pods without causing downtime.
Explain the use of `imagePullPolicy` in the context of updating pods.
Detail the steps to perform a rolling update of pods in a deployment.
Discuss the best practices for managing image updates in Kubernetes.
validation_target: Properly outline the steps and best practices for updating pods without downtime.

title: Debugging a Pending Pod
context: You are troubleshooting a Kubernetes cluster where pods are not starting.
task_steps: Identify the status of the pod using `kubectl get pods`.
Use `kubectl describe pod <pod-name>` to gather more details about the pod.
Check the resource requests against the available resources on the nodes using `kubectl describe node <node-name>`.
Look for taints on the nodes that might prevent the pod from scheduling using `kubectl get nodes --show-taints`.
Verify if there are any unschedulable nodes using `kubectl cordon`.
Ensure there are ready nodes available using `kubectl get nodes`.
validation_target: Correctly identify and resolve the issue causing the pod to be in a Pending state.

title: Debugging a ContainerCreating Pod
context: You are investigating a pod that is stuck in the ContainerCreating state.
task_steps: Use `kubectl describe pod <pod-name>` to gather more details about the pod.
Check for CNI errors by examining the pod's networking configuration.
Inspect volume mount failures by reviewing the pod's volume configurations.
Ensure the correct version of the CNI plugin is installed and configured.
Verify the external volumes are correctly attached to the node.
validation_target: Correctly identify and resolve the issue causing the pod to be in a ContainerCreating state.

title: Debugging a CrashLoopBackoff Pod
context: You are dealing with a pod that keeps restarting due to errors.
task_steps: Use `kubectl describe pod <pod-name>` to gather more details about the pod.
Review the logs of the pod using `kubectl logs <pod-name>`.

Modify the pod's command to `bash -c '
|| sleep 10d'` to allow debugging.
Enter a shell in the container using `kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -- bash`.
Check the health checks of the node using `kubectl get nodes`.
Pause and resume the rollout of the deployment using `kubectl rollout pause` and `kubectl rollout resume`.
validation_target: Correctly identify and resolve the issue causing the pod to enter a CrashLoopBackoff state.

title: Rolling Back a Deployment
context: You need to revert a previous change made to a Deployment.
task_steps: Apply the change to the Deployment with the `--record` flag.
Use `kubectl rollout history deployment <deployment-name>` to view the history.
Roll back to the previous version using `kubectl rollout undo deployment <deployment-name>`.
validation_target: Successfully roll back the Deployment to a previous version.

title: Understanding DaemonSet
context: You are learning about DaemonSets in Kubernetes.
task_steps: Read the documentation on DaemonSets: http://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/daemons/
validation_target: Correctly understand the purpose and usage of DaemonSets.

title: Understanding PetSet or StatefulSet
context: You are learning about PetSets and StatefulSets in Kubernetes.
task_steps: Read the documentation on PetSets: http://kubernetes.io/docs/user-guide/petset/
Read the documentation on StatefulSets: http://kubernetes.io/docs/tutorials/stateful-application/basic-stateful-set/
validation_target: Correctly understand the differences between PetSets and StatefulSets.

title: Understanding Ingress Controllers
context: You are setting up a new Kubernetes cluster and need to understand how Ingress Controllers work.
task_steps: Explain what an Ingress Controller is.
List at least three features of an Ingress Controller.
validation_target: Correctly describe the function and features of an Ingress Controller.

title: Troubleshooting Ingress Issues
context: During a recent deployment, you encountered 504 errors from your Ingress. You need to diagnose and fix the issue.
task_steps: Identify the cause of the 504 errors.
Propose a solution to prevent the 504 errors during deployments.
Explain why the proposed solution works.
validation_target: Correctly identify the root cause and propose an effective solution.

title: Explaining Kubernetes Services
context: You are explaining the concept of Kubernetes services to a colleague who is new to Kubernetes.
task_steps: Describe how a Kubernetes service works within the cluster.
Explain the difference between a ClusterIP and a Headless service.
validation_target: Correctly explain the functionality and differences between ClusterIP and Headless services.

title: Exposing Services to External Networks
context: Your team needs to expose a service to a host outside the Kubernetes cluster.
task_steps: Describe two methods to expose a service to external networks.
Explain the advantages and disadvantages of each method.
validation_target: Correctly describe and compare the methods of exposing services to external networks.

title: Understanding LoadBalancer Services
context: You are tasked with understanding how LoadBalancer services work in Kubernetes.
task_steps: Explain the default behavior of a LoadBalancer service.
Discuss the limitations of using a LoadBalancer service.
Propose a workaround for one of the limitations mentioned.
validation_target: Comprehensively explain the workings and limitations of LoadBalancer services and suggest improvements.

title: Node Affinity and Taints
context: You need to ensure that certain pods run on specific nodes in your Kubernetes cluster.
task_steps: Explain the concept of taints and node tolerations.

Run the tool on your project to identify any structs with uninitialized fields.

Modify the structs to initialize all fields appropriately.
validation_target: Identify and fix structs with uninitialized fields using go-exhaustruct.

validation_target: Identify and remove unreachable functions using deadcode.

title: Calculate Cyclomatic Complexity with gocyclo

validation_target: Identify functions and packages with high cyclomatic complexity

Review the output to identify packages with average complexity above the threshold
validation_target: Identify packages with average cyclomatic complexity above the threshold
